items.title: Global financial institutions are set to reduce exposure to the US Dollar: 4% of financial institutions plan to reduce their US Dollar exposure in the...
items.description_html:
Global financial institutions are set to reduce exposure to the US Dollar:

4% of financial institutions plan to reduce their US Dollar exposure in their portfolios over the next 12-24 months, the first such reading in 3 years, according to an OMFIF survey of 74 central banks and 16 public pension and sovereign wealth funds managing over $10 trillion in reserve assets.

The US Dollar is the only major currency expected to see a lower exposure among survey respondents.

Furthermore, 8% expect to reduce the US Dollar in their reserves over the next 10 years.

Nevertheless, the US Dollar is still the most held currency across financial institutions, at 58%, slightly down from 60% in 2025.

Meanwhile, 82% of central banks surveyed now hold physical gold, up from 71% last year.

51% of respondents cite protection against geopolitical risk as a motivation for holding gold, up from 40% in 2024.

Central banks are diversifying from the US Dollar into gold.

items.description_html:
Global farm production is set to grow over the next decade, but the benefits may remain deeply unequal.

By 2035, farm workers in rich countries could earn about 20 times more than those in South Asia and sub-Saharan Africa, according to the OECD-FAO Agricultural Outlook.

Annual farm worker incomes are projected at $22,155 in high-income countries compared with $1,100 in South Asia and sub-Saharan Africa, writes @shagun__k

items.title: RT Pratik Bhakta: UK-based cross-border remittance platform Aspora, which uses stable coins to transfer funds across international markets has picked ...
items.description_html:
RT Pratik Bhakta
UK-based cross-border remittance platform Aspora, which uses stable coins to transfer funds across international markets has picked up a 2% stake in Shivalik Small Finance Bank.

The fintech invested Rs 34crore for a 1.99% stake valuing the small finance bank at Rs 1,900 crore.

Interestingly the deal happened in February this year, but has so far gone under the radar.

Aspora, founded in Bengaluru but now headquartered in London, is backed by Sequoia US, Greylock, YCombinator and others.

Notable development given Aspora uses stable coin rails to transfer funds across borders faster and at cheaper rates. Last year the company claimed that its 250K users saved $15mn in fees by using crypto rails for cross-border remittance.

In India traditional financiers have mostly stayed away from crypto because of lack of regulations. Additionally tax authorities are understood to be scrutinising stablecoin-based remittance payments.

items.title: My new EAC-PM working paper (with Satvik Dev) argues that India’s rising solar penetration is causing grid stress. While solar is meeting more midday...
items.description_html:
My new EAC-PM working paper (with Satvik Dev) argues that India’s rising solar penetration is causing grid stress. While solar is meeting more midday demand, it also forces conventional sources, especially thermal plants, to ramp up and down more sharply to meet the net load. This is visible in both the summer “duck” curve and winter “camel” curve, putting grid operations under stress . Three signals show the stress. First, prices: in May 2026, power on the IEX day-ahead market averaged Rs 1.11 per unit at midday but Rs 9.71 at night. Second, curtailment: about 24 GWh of solar was wasted daily in May. Third, shortages: the grid fell short of non-solar-hour peak demand on 36 days in April-May, compared with only 6 days for solar-hour peak demand. The paper supports the direction of the Draft Electricity (Amendment) Bill, 2025, and draft Electricity (Rights of Consumers) Amendment Rules, 2026. But it cautions that without adequate storage and net-load smoothing policies, solar growth will intensify grid stress.

https://eacpm.gov.in/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/2026.07.03_Working_Paper_Current.pdf

items.title: India's fintech sector entering a phase where trust & governance matter as much as growth & innovation. •59% of respondents rank reputation & brand r...
items.description_html:
India's fintech sector entering a phase where trust & governance matter as much as growth & innovation.
•59% of respondents rank reputation & brand risk as top concern
•51% flag interoperability & infrastructure risk, tied to growing reliance on UPI, Aadhaar & other digital public infrastructure
•Market competition & conduct risk ran 3rd with average severity score of 6.9
•Data access, privacy & protection score 6.6, with 49% calling it high severity
•Cybersecurity & business continuity scored 6.5, with 46% rating it high severity
•AI/ML & model risk score lowest at 5.8. This will climb sharply as AI adoption deepens in underwriting and fraud detection.
FACE & Grant Thornton Bharat Fintech survey of 39 FACE member fintechs spanning lending, payments, regtech, collection-tech & techfins

Tam for EMS industry

India is emerging as a key global electronics manufacturing hub, with the EMS industry expected to grow at 25-30% CAGR over FY24-29E.

Growth is driven by the China+1 shift, electronics exports reaching ~USD48b in FY26 (+25% YoY), and policy support through PLI, INR400b ECMS and semiconductor initiatives.

With India accounting for only 4-5% of the global EMS market, increasing component localization and export-led manufacturing provide a long runway for growth.

EMS companies which will go backwards into component manufacturing are the ones where complete integrated business models will be created

items.title: Jewellery Retailers : Quarterly updates Summary - Q1FY27 REV growth - Senco Gold - 60% - Titan - 41% - P N Gadgil - 41% - Kalyan Jewellers - 38% Kalya...
items.description_html:
Jewellery Retailers : Quarterly updates

Summary - Q1FY27 REV growth
- Senco Gold - 60%
- Titan - 41%
- P N Gadgil - 41%
- Kalyan Jewellers - 38%

Kalyan Jewellers delivered REV growth of 38% YoY

India Operations - Growth ~38% & SSSG ~28%

International Opns - Growth ~30% & Middle East ~30%

Candere reported 112% growth

During the quarter it added 12 Kalyan showrooms & 5 Candere showrooms in India

Total store count stood at 524 as of June'26

Titan reported strong Q1FY27 update

Consumer businesses grew 41% YoY
Jewellery business grew 39% YoY

Tanishq, Mia, Zoya, beYon grew 39% YoY, while CaratLane grew 42% YoY

Buyer growth was in early double digits, while average ticket size grew in high double digits

Watches business grew 23% YoY & EyeCare grew 23% YoY

International business grew 128% YoY, partly supported by Damas consolidation from Jan’26

77 net store additions during the quarter, taking the total store count to 3,680

P N Gadgil delivered robust Q1FY27, with REV growth of 41% YoY

Retail Segment - Growth ~56% & SSSG ~46%

Retail contributed 78% of total REV, which is meaningfully higher on a YoY basis

Franchise business grew 8% YoY, while e-commerce business grew 20% YoY

Gold bullion share in retail REV has normalised to 22%

Studded jewellery mix improved, with retail stud ratio rising to 10.9%

Total store count stood at 78 as of June 2026

For FY27, it remains on track to achieve the FY27 guidance & open 25 new stores, taking total store count to around 103 by the end of FY27

Senco Gold delivered 60% REV growth, while retail REV growth stood at 48% YoY

SSSG came at 38% YoY, supported by Akshaya Tritiya, Poila Baisakh, Baishakhi, Bihu & summer wedding season

Added 8 new showrooms in Q1FY27, taking the total network to 208 showrooms

It plans to open 12-15 more showrooms over the next 3 quarters

Titan’s Q1 update came as a positive surprise, street is taking it positively since the growth has been stellar despite the high base

On the other hand, Kalyan Jewellers which had consistently outperformed peers in earlier quarters, has seen some growth moderation this quarter

Overall, jewellery retailers have reported strong Q1FY27 updates. Growth has been supported by festive demand, Akshaya Tritiya, higher gold prices, strong SSSG & store additions.

items.description_html:
MCX is down around 25% from its recent highs

It delivered explosive growth over the last few quarters

FY26 was a dream year for MCX as Revenue doubled, PAT crossed 1,300 Cr

Higher bullion volatility drove trading activity, options volumes exploded & operating leverage played out beautifully.

When gold & silver prices move sharply, participation increases, hedging activity rises & speculative volumes also pick up. That is where MCX benefits the most.

Earnings are driven more by trading volumes & volatility, so if the volatile phase in commodity normalizes, growth rates can also moderate.

items.title: RT Sandeep Parekh: The idea that we must achieve perfect urban nirvana before we’re allowed to build anything decent is how countries stay stuck. You...
items.description_html:
RT Sandeep Parekh
The idea that we must achieve perfect urban nirvana before we’re allowed to build anything decent is how countries stay stuck. You don’t fix a messy house by refusing to clean any room until every single one is spotless. You start somewhere. Sometimes that somewhere has jet bridges and decent coffee.

Also, the dramatic “we are a danger to ourselves” line is doing a lot of heavy lifting. India has problems, nobody’s denying that. But pretending that shiny new airports are the problem instead of one small part of the solution is just elegant cope dressed up as insight.
Classic case of “I saw one bad thing, therefore all good things are fake.”

items.description_html:
Recognition as one of India's Top 5 Best Companies to Work For becomes even more credible when employee-first decisions continue throughout the year.

Flipkart's latest ESOP liquidity event gives employees another opportunity to liquidate a percentage of their vested stock options, reinforcing a culture where growth is shared across the organisation. The best employee benefits aren't always free meals or fancy offices. Sometimes, they're wealth creation opportunities.

At a time when such opportunities remain uncommon, Flipkart continues to be one of the few large internet companies in India to consistently provide employees with regular ESOPs, allowing them to unlock the value they help create.

Like i always say, Salary makes a living, Equity makes a killing! Hope more firms allow wealth creation via ESOPs for their employees! Transform employees into partners via ESOPs!

items.title: SOLID Commentary by SWIGGY in recent Roadshows Can SWIGGY RE-RATE NOW ? IIFL recommends buy with target of Rs 480 * In Food Delivery, new offerings ar...
items.description_html:
SOLID Commentary by SWIGGY in recent Roadshows

Can SWIGGY RE-RATE NOW ?

IIFL recommends buy with target of Rs 480

* In Food Delivery, new offerings are aimed at expanding category adoption, and management remains confident of sustaining healthy growth while maintaining profitability. Margin expansion from here is expected from operating leverage rather than CM.

* QC - Quick Commerce remains focused on achieving profitable growth through better monetization, retention and network utilization. Existing infra can support growth through better utilization, while CM breakeven is expected in 1QFY27.

* Highlighted the importance of proprietary brands, premium assortments and innovation-led customer engagement in strengthening long-term competitive positioning.

* Dineout is already at ~1% margin and is targeted to reach ~5% on USD1bn of GOV within two years.

* They remain comfortable with their balance sheet, expecting FD profitability and treasury to increasingly offset QC investments. WC intensity should decline in QC as the network matures.

items.title: RT Poe Zhao: China's MIIT says humanoid robot production will exceed 100,000 units this year, and robots are "entering factories and workshops." This ...
items.description_html:
RT Poe Zhao
China's MIIT says humanoid robot production will exceed 100,000 units this year, and robots are "entering factories and workshops."

This is the transition from spectacle to economics I documented at the end of 2025. The industry proved it could manufacture publicity. Now it has to manufacture profits. 100,000 units confirms the supply chain has matured. The open question is whether deployed robots can beat the RMB 150,000-a-year factory worker on a spreadsheet. Production volume and deployment viability are two different races, and most of the industry is still running the first one.

items.title: Samsung Electronics could post its highest-ever profit this year. Riding the ongoing AI boom, the company could generate more operating profit this ye...
items.description_html:
Samsung Electronics could post its highest-ever profit this year.

Riding the ongoing AI boom, the company could generate more operating profit this year than its cumulative operating profit over the past 40 years!

Operating profit for the full year could reach around KRW 300 trillion ($217.4 billion). If Samsung achieves those figures, it could surpass Nvidia’s Q1 2026 operating profit of $59.2 billion, making it one of the world’s most profitable companies

Samsung Electronics acquired Korea Semiconductor in 1974 and began manufacturing integrated circuits (ICs) for LED wristwatches. It later introduced its first 64Kb DRAM chip in 1983 and began selling them in 1984. The company’s cumulative operating profit from 1985 to 2025 was less than KRW 300 trillion (~$217.4 billion).

Samsung plans to invest more than KRW 40 trillion (~$29 billion) annually to meet growing demand for AI semiconductors.

AI/Semiconductor Boom continues!

items.title: US small-cap companies are facing a massive interest burden: Interest expense now accounts for 31% of EBITDA in Russell 2000 firms, the highest in at ...
items.description_html:
US small-cap companies are facing a massive interest burden:

Interest expense now accounts for 31% of EBITDA in Russell 2000 firms, the highest in at least 6 years.

This percentage has more than doubled since 2020.

By comparison, S&P 500 companies now carry interest expense equal to 6.7% of EBITDA, down from 9.5% in 2020.

This comes as ~30% of corporate debt in Russell 2000 companies is tied to floating rates, compared to ~7% for the S&P 500.

Meanwhile, a near-record ~40% of small-cap companies in the index remain unprofitable.

Small-cap firms are in desperate need of lower rates.

items.title: Stress in the US private credit market is intensifying: Investors requested a record -$15.6 billion in redemptions from private credit funds in Q2 202...
items.description_html:
Stress in the US private credit market is intensifying:

Investors requested a record -$15.6 billion in redemptions from private credit funds in Q2 2026.

This marks the 3rd consecutive quarterly increase by a total of +$13 billion, or +500%.

Furthermore, just 38% of these requests were met, down from 53% in Q1 2026, leaving $9.7 billion in unmet redemptions, the largest backlog on record.

Blue Owl's flagship fund, Blue Owl Credit Income, was the most impacted at 19% of shares outstanding, with 14% unmet, the highest redemption rate among its large competitors.

This was followed by Apollo, at 16% requested with 11% unmet, and Ares, at 14% requested with 9% unmet.

Meanwhile, inflows into the private credit industry declined -75% since January to ~$500 million in May, the smallest monthly intake in at least 18 months.

The private credit crisis shows no signs of slowing.

items.title: Foreign debt demand is no longer keeping up with America's growing debt: Foreign official holdings of US Treasuries are down to 12.5% of total Treasur...
items.description_html:
Foreign debt demand is no longer keeping up with America's growing debt:

Foreign official holdings of US Treasuries are down to 12.5% of total Treasuries outstanding, the lowest this century.

This percentage has declined -24 points since the 2009 peak.

Over this period, marketable US Treasury debt has surged +$23 trillion, or +379%, to $29.1 trillion, near an all-time high.

At the same time, US Treasuries held by foreign government entities have increased by just +$1.5 trillion, or +63%, to ~$3.9 trillion.

China's Treasury holdings have more than halved, to $651.1 billion, the lowest since September 2008.

The US is issuing record levels of debt.

items.title: US consumer sentiment points to further job market weakness: The gap between consumers saying jobs are "plentiful" versus "hard to find" fell to just ...
items.description_html:
US consumer sentiment points to further job market weakness:

The gap between consumers saying jobs are "plentiful" versus "hard to find" fell to just 2.4 points in June, the lowest since the 2020 pandemic.

Just 24.9% of consumers now say jobs are "plentiful," down from ~55.0% in 2022, while 22.5% say jobs are "hard to find," up from ~10.0% over the same period, and the highest since January 2021.

Historically, this measure has been one of the most reliable leading indicators of rising unemployment, and it now suggests the US unemployment rate could rise to as high as 6.0%, from the current 4.2%.

Meanwhile, the labor force participation rate, which measures the working-age population of those either employed or looking for a job, fell to 61.5% in June, the lowest since June 1976, excluding the pandemic period.

This comes as the labor force dropped -720,000 last month, to 169.36 million, the lowest since December 2024.

The job market is much weaker than headlines suggest.

items.description_html:
Investor sentiment in the energy market is rapidly shifting:

Energy funds posted -$3.2 billion in outflows in the week ending July 1st, the largest weekly withdrawal since July 2024.

This is also the 2nd-biggest weekly outflow in at least 10 years.

Energy funds also saw -$1.5 billion in outflows the prior week, the largest since April 2025.

As a result, the 4-week average of outflows is up to -$1.8 billion, the biggest on record.

This marks a sharp reversal from a record +$2.5 billion in the 4-week average of inflows seen just 2 months ago.

Investors are aggressively rotating out of the energy sector.

items.title: We now believe the S&P 500 is setting up for 8,000+. Here's why: Chip stocks have quietly become the new leaders of this bull market. While many of th...
items.description_html:
We now believe the S&P 500 is setting up for 8,000+. Here's why:

Chip stocks have quietly become the new leaders of this bull market.

While many of the Magnificent 7 stocks have declined 20%+ from their recent highs, semiconductor names have taken over.

In fact, 8 of the 10 best-performing S&P 500 stocks this year are in the chip industry.

As a result, the S&P 500 now sits just ~1% below a record high despite weakness the big tech stocks which have led the market since 2022.

This marks the first time since 2022 that the market traded highly with leadership outside of the Magnificent 7, while the Magnificent 7 traded in the opposite direction.

In our view, this rotation is constructive as the Magnificent 7 prepares to "sub back in."

As large-cap tech begins to regain leadership while semiconductors continue to outperform or even cool-off, the S&P 500 is poised for 8,000+.

Asset owners will continue to win.

items.title: This tweet has been coming on my timeline few times now. It's unfortunate, but have three of my learnings on this to share. : 1. Concentrated investin...
items.description_html:
This tweet has been coming on my timeline few times now. It's unfortunate, but have three of my learnings on this to share. :

1. Concentrated investing is a double edged sword - It is not for everyone and doesn't work in 9 out of 10 cases. Most of us would have read stories of wealth creation from concentration, but the truth is that a large part of it is survivorship bias. When you have a super concentrated portfolio, you must be prepared for deep cuts, and frameworks for risk control.

2. Mere cheapness is not a reason to buy - If something is trading "cheap" or below past median valuations, often there is a reason. Growth is the cornerstone of good investing - valuation is secondary. Most investors need better framework of valuations, linked to future and not the past (P/E). Assuming that the market is wrong and you are right is market arrogance.

"It’s pretty hard in a declining business to buy things cheap enough to compensate for the decline."
- Warren Buffett

3. Capacity to suffer - Losses are notional till they are realized. Avoid permanent capital erosion at all costs. Avoid F&O, avoid turnaround bets, avoid investing without manage comfort. All other losses can be recovered. Hence capacity to suffer becomes paramount for investors and also businesses.

Those were my two cents. Happy investing to all.

items.title: CHART OF THE DAY: Saudi Arabia has only offered its flagship Arab Light for Far East delivery at minus $1.5 a barrel (or cheaper) twice in the last 25...
items.description_html:
CHART OF THE DAY: Saudi Arabia has only offered its flagship Arab Light for Far East delivery at minus $1.5 a barrel (or cheaper) twice in the last 25 years: once during the 2015 price war against US shale, and again in 2020 during the price war against Russia. That's it.


Javier Blas: Saudi Arabia slashes the official selling price of its flagship Arab Light, pricing the barrels for Asia delivery at **minus** $1.5 a barrel to the Dubai benchmark.

It's the largest **discount** for Arab Light to Asia since mid-2020, when the world was still battling Covid-19.

items.title: Noah Smith gets much right in his argument as to how China managed the huge property-bubble deflation in a "painless" way, and why it's a little silly...
items.description_html:
Noah Smith gets much right in his argument as to how China managed the huge property-bubble deflation in a "painless" way, and why it's a little silly to say that Beijing managed to deflate a massive housing bubble at no cost. The key point, as he notes, is that whenever China's faces an economic shock, Beijing "uses its direct control over the banking system to push banks to lend more."

What's more, contrary to some of the more dizzy comments to which his essay is a response, China is not the first country to have figured out how to manage adverse economic shocks by setting of compensating investment bubbles. Many investment-driven economies have done the same, most notoriously the US in the mid 1920s, Latin America in the early and mid 1970s (in response to the oil shocks), and Japan in the mid 1980s.

What this means in practice is that the system is not allowed to adjust. Each economic shock is countered by setting off a new investment bubble. For example the post-2009 collapse in China's current account surplus (from 10%of GDP to 3% of GDP) was balanced by inflating an infrastructure bubble.

At the time this was hailed as a brilliant move by Chinese and (especially) foreign economists, although today many Chinese economists will tell you (mostly in private, but occasionally even in public) that this was a mistake, and was the beginning of China's debt problem.

The reason is that if China had been severely underinvested in infrastructure, balancing the contraction in trade with an expansion in infrastructure investment would have been both clever and sustainable, but by 2009 China already had almost as much infrastructure as it could productively absorb, and so the surge in infrastructure investment was decided on not because China needed more infrastructure but because a slowdown would have been more disruptive than China could absorb.

Consider what happened next. As China tried to bring infrastructure investment levels down in the mid-2010s, it could only do so "without pain" by setting off what turned out to be the last stage of the property bubble. It did this in 2015-16 by eliminating many property purchase restrictions, lowering minimum payments, reducing mortgage rates, and forcing banks to expand mortgage lending (often in spite of growing concerns among banks).

These policies, not surprisingly, set off a new wave of surging home prices and expanding property development, but this couldn't last long, and when the property sector, gorged on almost unimaginable levels of debt, began its sharp contraction in 2021-22, Beijing once again responded by setting off an investment bubble in another sector. This time, as Smith notes, it set if off in the manufacturing sector, with the decline in property investment matched almost dollar for dollar with an increase in manufacturing investment.

Once again, if China had been severely underinvested in manufacturing, this would have been a great move, but because at the time it was already over-reliant on a very large manufacturing sector (even larger than the property sector at its height) and suffering from excess manufacturing capacity, the result within 2-3 years was massive increases in excess capacity, severe involution, and an uncontrollable surge in the trade surplus.

"So what?" some of the less historically-literate might wonder. If every time an investment bubble contracts, and the authorities can negate the economic impact by setting off an investment bubble in another sector, shouldn't they keep doing it forever?

Yes, if they can but, for what should be obvious reasons, they cannot, and the longer they postpone the adjustment, the more costly it is likely to be. The problem, of course, is that each bubble has to be bigger than the last to keep economic activity from slowing. We can see this most obviously in the acceleration in the growth of the country's debt burden, already the second highest in the world (after Japan's) and the fastest-growing in history.

To the extent that debt funds productive investment, of course, rising debt should not translate into a rising debt-to-GDP ratio, but in an economy in which nearly all debt funds investment, and very little funds consumption or transfers, many years of a rapidly-rising debt-to-GDP ratio is pretty strong evidence that the investment is increasingly unproductive.

Among other things this means that a bubble-replacement strategy can look very clever in the short term, but it can only run on as long as growth in the debt burden can accelerate.


Noah Smith 🐇🇺🇸🇺🇦🇹🇼: Some people think China avoided an economic crash after its property bubble burst.

But it did not.

https://www.noahpinion.blog/p/no-china-did-not-manage-to-avoid

items.title: Auto Ancillaries: Premiumisation Driving Higher Content Per Vehicle Rising consumer demand for premium features is increasing the value of components ...
items.description_html:
Auto Ancillaries: Premiumisation Driving Higher Content Per Vehicle

Rising consumer demand for premium features is increasing the value of components per vehicle, creating a structural growth driver for auto ancillary companies.
Connected features are expected to see the fastest adoption, rising from 2% (FY19) to 50% (FY27E).
Sunroof penetration is projected to increase from 10% to 40% over FY19–FY27E.
Automatic transmission (AT) adoption is expected to grow from 17% to 35%, while alloy wheels rise to 50% penetration.
LED lighting penetration is also expected to increase from 20% to 35% by FY27E.
Higher feature content translates into higher vehicle ASPs and greater revenue opportunity for component manufacturers, even if overall vehicle volumes grow at a moderate pace.

items.title: Indo Borax: ₹20 Cr Boron Oxide Capex to Move Up the Value Chain Indo Borax is building its next phase of growth around 4 clear priorities — Boric Ac...
items.description_html:
Indo Borax: ₹20 Cr Boron Oxide Capex to Move Up the Value Chain

Indo Borax is building its next phase of growth around 4 clear priorities — Boric Acid leadership, Boron Oxide expansion, exports and R&D.

- Company will continue to strengthen its leadership in Boric Acid

- Focus remains on capacity enhancement and operational improvements

- Indo Borax is investing ~₹20 crore in a new Boron Oxide facility at Pithampur

- Planned Boron Oxide capacity: 4,000 MTPA

- This will help the company move into higher-margin Boron chemistry products

- International expansion is another key priority

- Focus markets include South America and South-East Asia

- R&D investments will be increased for specialised boron-based solutions

- Target applications include advanced materials, flame retardants and specialty agriculture

Key takeaway:

Indo Borax is trying to evolve from a Boric Acid-led company into a broader Boron chemistry platform.

The ₹20 crore Boron Oxide capex is small in size, but strategically important — it pushes the company towards higher-margin products and newer global markets.


Beat The Street: Indo Borax - Leader in Boric Acid

An import substitution play on new and emerging chemical products - Expanding Products and Integration

Going through a strategic transformation

- Ganesha Ecosphere recycles 850 Cr scrap bottles every year! That's 2.33 Cr bottles every day and almost 10L bottles every day - This is just 25%, so India needs to recycle 3500 cr scrap plastic bottles
- There could be more plastic than fish in the ocean by 2050
- Only 9% of all plastic produced is recycled in the World
- One million plastic bottles are bought every minute!
- 50000+ soft drink bottles consumed per minute, 1.8 cr tons of plastic waste per year

Recycling is key now! no stock reco

Supriya Lifescience is down 10% today

The fall is mainly due to a disclosure regarding judicial proceedings involving one of the company’s personnel

Proceedings have been initiated against Mr. Sreekant Sreedharan, General Manager - Sales & Marketing, by the Special Investigation & Intelligence Branch of the Customs Department

The matter is under the NDPS Act, 1985 & relates to a procedural lapse in a single export transaction of an identified product

The company has clarified that, based on the information currently available, the proceedings are not expected to have any material impact on its operations

However, since the matter involves customs, market seems to be reacting cautiously

For now, this is not a business performance issue, but a governance / compliance related issue

items.title: RT Nimesh Shah: Brokerages on Grid Cos after sharp correction on Friday While Nomura Says - Overreaction to narrow exemption CLSA believes - Increased...
items.description_html:
RT Nimesh Shah
Brokerages on Grid Cos after sharp correction on Friday

While Nomura Says - Overreaction to narrow exemption
CLSA believes - Increased competition (ex-HVDC converters) is likely to weigh on pricing power & margins of domestic T&D equipment cos & Investors should be wary of lofty vals(PE of 50-90x)
@ShereenBhan
@_prashantnair @Reematendulkar
@CNBCTV18News @CNBCTV18Live
Detailed note 👇
Nomura on Industrials
Overreaction to narrow exemption
Order explicitly states it creates no precedent
Market players read it as a competitive threat & sold off listed power-equipment stocks such as - GE Vernova T&D India (Buy), CG Power (Buy), Hitachi
Energy Not rated) & Siemens Energy Not rated)
Exempted players won 9% of transmission-linked PGCIL tenders over FY09-20
Uneven capacity readiness across four entities: only one appears to be running near-optimal utilization
Exemption is explicitly sunset-bound, & grid infrastructure sits squarely in national-security bucket
Two-year window is too short to underwrite meaningful capacity expansion in India

Jefferies on Industrials
Media reports highlight that government has approved four Chinese companies (TBEA Energy, Nanjing Electric, New Northeast Electric, Taikai Electric) to supply equipment for government projects only from their Indian manufacturing facilities.
Import restrictions still remain.
Even with supply from these facilities, believe demand-supply mismatch remains.
Remain positive on Hitachi Energy and Siemens Energy and see the correction as a buying opportunity.

Macquarie on Industrials
As per industry sources, government is set to exempt 4 Chinese players (detailed below), having manufacturing units in India, from the restriction imposed in February 2023 for critical power projects.
This exemption is valid for a period of two years (till June 2028)
Most of grid companies are in midst of capacity expansion and are likely to add capacity over the next 2-3 year, which is the validity of this exemption.
Should this exemption be to extend in future, long-term negative impact on margins of the incumbents, especially the 765kV GIS players cannot be ruled out
Barring TBEA, most of other companies have sub-scale operations in India & are likely to take time to ramp up
Believe this exemption is to address current shortage of certain of EHV grid equipment & unlikely to allow large scale Chinese imports in sensitive areas of grid infrastructure.

CLSA on Industrials
Indian government has granted a two-year exemption to four Chinese electrical equipment manufacturers with manufacturing units in India, allowing them to participate in government/CPSE tenders.
More important is that this exemption was granted at request of Ministry of Power, validating case led by complaints from transmission utilities struggling with price inflation on controlled supply chains
Do not see this move impacting near-term volume growth, but increased competition (ex-HVDC converters) is likely to weigh on pricing power & margins of domestic T&D equipment cos such as Hitachi, GE Vernova T&D, BHEL (U-PF) & CG Power
Investors should be wary of lofty valuations (PE of 50-90x) on historic peak high margins led by ‘Make in India” driven shield but now govt seems to have taken cognisance of excessive margins being made by transformer sector.

items.title: RT David Senra: Groq Founder @JonathanRoss321 explains why all the West Coast VCs missed on investing in Groq: “Typical West Coast VCs are more like ...
items.description_html:
RT David Senra
Groq Founder @JonathanRoss321 explains why all the West Coast VCs missed on investing in Groq:

“Typical West Coast VCs are more like lemmings. Ttypical East Coast VCs all think that they're smarter than each other.”

“So when you try and raise from the West Coast, if one VC puts money in, all the others want to put money in.”

“In New York, one VC investing means nothing. They're going to run their own analysis. They do not care what other VCs are doing.”

“The flip of that is, if you're on the West Coast and one VC passes, they're going to go tell every other VC, and like lemmings, they're all going to pass as well.”

“All the VCs on the West Coast didn't want to invest in us. We had very few of the typical VCs invest in us at the end. We had a bunch of crossover funds from the East Coast investing.”


David Senra: A conversation with @JonathanRoss321, founder of @GroqInc. This is the first podcast that Jonathan talks about his $20 billion partnership with NVIDIA.

In this conversation, we talk about his decade building Groq: why West Coast VCs kept passing on the company, how fast Jensen

items.description_html:
BREAKING: US technology funds attracted +$14.3 billion in inflows in the week ending July 1st, the 2nd biggest weekly inflow on record.

This follows -$9.3 billion in outflows the prior week and a record +$19.2 billion in inflows two weeks earlier.

This brings the 4-week average of weekly inflows to a record +$9.0 billion.

Tech funds are now on pace to attract +$152 billion in inflows in 2026, an all-time high.

Meanwhile, US equity funds recorded -$17.2 billion in outflows in the week ending July 1st, the largest weekly withdrawal since March.

Investors are aggressively rotating into technology.

items.title: Shocking stat of the day: The US labor force participation rate among 25 to 54-year-olds dropped -0.6 percentage points in June, the 2nd-largest month...
items.description_html:
Shocking stat of the day:

The US labor force participation rate among 25 to 54-year-olds dropped -0.6 percentage points in June, the 2nd-largest monthly decline on record in data going back to the 1940s.

This metric measures the proportion of Americans in prime age either working or actively looking for work.

The decline matches those seen in January 1968, March 1960, May 1953, October 1952, and June 1951.

The only larger monthly drop was recorded in April 2020, during the pandemic, when nearly the entire economy shut down.

As a result, the prime-age labor force participation rate fell to 83.3%, the lowest since December 2023.

Meanwhile, the total labor force participation rate has declined for 7 consecutive months, to 61.5%, the lowest since February 2021.

The US job market is rapidly deteriorating under the surface.

items.description_html:
RT The West Bengal Index
𝗥𝗮𝗯𝗶𝗻𝗱𝗿𝗮 𝗦𝗮𝗿𝗼𝗯𝗮𝗿 𝘁𝗼 𝗴𝗲𝘁 𝗺𝗮𝗷𝗼𝗿 𝗿𝗲𝘃𝗮𝗺𝗽 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗢𝗹𝘆𝗺𝗽𝗶𝗰 𝘁𝗿𝗮𝗰𝗸.

Kolkata's historic Rabindra Sarobar Stadium is set for a comprehensive redevelopment aimed at bringing top-tier domestic football and athletics back to the venue. Plans include an Olympic-standard athletics track, upgraded athlete facilities, restored galleries and improved infrastructure. The project will also remove illegal encroachments and unauthorised structures inside the stadium premises.

After inspecting the venue, officials from the Government of West Bengal described its condition as deeply unfortunate, citing collapsed galleries, neglected infrastructure, poor athlete amenities and overgrown vegetation. Redevelopment will be carried out while protecting the ecological balance of the Rabindra Sarobar area, with biodiversity conservation remaining a key priority.

items.title: The AI spending boom is redefining the US economy: AI CapEx across Alphabet, $GOOGL, Amazon, $AMZN, Meta, $META, Microsoft, $MSFT, and Oracle, $ORCL, ...
items.description_html:
The AI spending boom is redefining the US economy:

AI CapEx across Alphabet, $GOOGL, Amazon, $AMZN, Meta, $META, Microsoft, $MSFT, and Oracle, $ORCL, is projected to rise to ~3.2% of US GDP in 2027.

If that materializes, annual CapEx will surpass national defense spending for the first time in history, which is projected at ~2.7% of GDP next year.

For this year alone, AI CapEx is set to increase to ~2.5% of GDP, from 1.5% in 2025, close to the estimated defense spending of ~2.7%.

This comes as AI CapEx of these 5 companies is projected to surpass $800 billion in 2026.

Subsequently, in 2027, this is expected to rise further, to a record $1.1 trillion.

Truly mind-blowing numbers.

items.title: RT menaka doshi: Why does a country that gave the world the concept of zero now invest so little in creating new knowledge? There is no shortage of ex...
items.description_html:
RT menaka doshi
Why does a country that gave the world the concept of zero now invest so little in creating new knowledge? There is no shortage of explanations.

Chief Economic Adviser V. Anantha Nageswaran has pointed to colonial deindustrialization, decades of protectionism, premature financialization and policy uncertainty. Industrialist Naushad Forbes argues the deeper problem is insufficient competition, which has reduced the pressure on companies to innovate.

Ajai Chowdhry, co-founder of HCL Infosystems, tells me it’s because India has failed to become a product nation.

“When you design and make products in India, that’s when you will see R&D happen. In service businesses, people don’t invest in deep research,” said Chowdhry, who now serves as chairman of the National Quantum Mission.

Lessons From India’s Big Antibiotic Breakthrough 👇

https://www.bloomberg.com/news/newsletters/2026-07-02/indian-innovation-shows-proof-of-life-with-wockhardt-s-antibiotic-breakthrough?srnd=undefined

In 2025, it corrected more than 50%.

There were significant concerns & allegations surrounding the company back then

Again in 2026, it corrected by almost 35%

This is how brutal volatility can be in equities particularly smallcaps

These sharp movements will keep happening

You name any stock, the journey will be full of volatility

If you want to make money in equities, you will have to make volatility your friend, not your enemy

End result is very rewarding, but corrections often throw investors out if they do not have the patience & conviction to hold through volatility

That is why the focus must be on the business rather than just the stock price

If the business is moving in the right direction, sooner or later the stock price usually follows the same trajectory

First initiated Yatharth when it was close to 350, have been riding it since then, while pyramiding along the way. Back then, the company operated 4 hospitals & now it has scaled to 9 hospitals. It has made a lot of progress over the years, guidance also looks interesting

Equity investors make a lot of money over the long term because the downside is limited, but the upside is limitless

That is the fun part of equity investing !


Equity Insights Elite: Yatharth ATH🔥

<450 : a brokerage stopped coverage, income tax raids, corporate governance concerns, expensive valuations…

Negativity sells fast

Now everything is fine!

items.title: Few thoughts on Value added products here
items.description_html:
Few thoughts on Value added products here


Intrinsic Compounding: Why you should take notice when a management starts talking about Value Added Products?

1. Suppose you run a Dosa Tiffin shop and sell plain Dosa for Rs 50. You are doing well and make a name for yourself. Customers start demanding Masala Dosa, Podi Ghee Dosa & Rava dosa from

items.title: Why you should take notice when a management starts talking about Value Added Products? 1. Suppose you run a Dosa Tiffin shop and sell plain Dosa for ...
items.description_html:
Why you should take notice when a management starts talking about Value Added Products?

1. Suppose you run a Dosa Tiffin shop and sell plain Dosa for Rs 50. You are doing well and make a name for yourself. Customers start demanding Masala Dosa, Podi Ghee Dosa & Rava dosa from you. You include them in your Menu at a price point that is double of a Plain Masala Dosa. This leads to better realisations and higher margins for you.

In a nutshell you have released a value added product for your Tiffin shop :)

2. Just think of a manufacturing business which has different customers and they are expert at manufacturing Stainless Steel Pipes, and are mainly catering to chemical customers. Over a period of time, they realise that by getting more certifications & by putting more machines. They can easily increase the number of industries they cater to but only challenge is to go through audits to get the certificates & the time involved in setting up new machines.

They go through this cycle and start catering to higher barrier to entry industries like Nuclear, Data centres and Aerospace. This is called climbing the value chain and getting into adjacencies which ends up making your business more robust.

3. Whenever, a business starts talking about Value Added products. Always sit up and take notice. Let me give you a few examples-

A) In 2018 APL Apollo started talking about focusing on Value Added products like Heavy structural tubes. They were making an EBITDA per tonne of Rs 3000. Post introducing new value added products like Apollo Chaukhat, Steel planks, and DFT steel tubes. The EBITDA per tonne has increased from Rs 3000 to Rs5600 as of Q4FY26. This is how value added products add directly to EBITDA for a company.

B) Recently, Venus pipes which is a SS tubes manufacturer has won a LOI of 185 crores in Data Centre spooling. This is an example of how a company is going beyond the traditional industries it serves into something new where peers like Ratnamani in Nuclear spooling do margins upwards of 30% mark.

C) Another example is that of Goodluck India which has diversified away from core steel tubes business into manufacturing of 155mm Artillery shells. This is addition of a vertical where value addition and margins are much higher due to shortage of shells in the world. Base business makes margins of 8-10%, and new business will have margins north of 25%+.

D) A lot of Pharma CDMO businesses started out as API manufacturing and have steadily climbed the value chain to become Contract manufactures for innovators. Eg- Neulands margins went from 14-15% to 28% in this journey or how Laurus & Acutaas have steadily increased the mix of CDMO in their overall revenues. This journey of getting into a higher barrier to entry products is often full of lumpiness & volatility. This is something what the likes of AARTI Pharma or Granules are trying i.e. to increase the mix of CDMO in the overall business.

E) Finally, an example of a company where value addition can improve. Yash high voltage manufactures OIP and RIP bushings. In RIP bushings the realisations are 3-4x that of OIP bushings. Think of Bushings as a mission critical component used in transformers which constitutes 3% of the cost. Yash High is trying to backwards integrate into their biggest cost line item: Cores used for manufacturing Rip Bushings. This will be close to 10% of BOM for a RIP bushing. If they crack it, then the value addition per product will go up and import dependency will finish.

4. In conclusion:

A) Look out when managements are trying to enter higher barrier to entry product verticals.

B) Check if Gross Profit & Ebitda margins for a company have started improving, and look if the reason for the improvement is due to higher value addition or value added products.

C) Look out for words like: Value added products, climbing the value chain, higher margin products, new applications, backward integration etc in the concall. This will indicate if the business is looking to climb up the value chain or not :)

This is it for today. Hopefully, you loved the mental model of: Value added products.

Disclaimer: No recommendation to buy or sell. Pure idea is to teach you different mental models used in business analysis.

items.title: RT Madhav Chanchani: Another interesting point here is Peter Thiel's ability to read market cycles and knowing when not to invest. At the peak of the ...
items.description_html:
RT Madhav Chanchani
Another interesting point here is Peter Thiel's ability to read market cycles and knowing when not to invest.

At the peak of the 2021 bubble, he wanted the Founders Fund team to "go to the beach" for a year and stop investing.

But he knew the team would still want to deploy capital because the market was so hot.

So instead, he told them to identify the two or three best companies, invest at whatever the market-clearing price was, and ignore everything else.

One of those investments was Ramp, where Founders Fund invested at a $9 billion post-money valuation. Nine months later, Ramp had to raise a down round at $6 billion valuation.

But today, however, Ramp is worth around $40 billion - showing that backing good companies even if the timing is not ideal works out.

"I think there's something really unique about Peter. He understands that it's less important to be exactly right than to be directionally right, because it's really hard to shift the Overton window. Most people naturally moderate their views, and because of that they don't push hard enough to change strategy. Peter is different. He'll dig in his heels—not necessarily because he believes every word literally, but because he's trying to move the window, even if it's only a little. That small shift in thinking ends up being incredibly powerful," according to Trae Stephens of Founders Fund.


Madhav Chanchani: Partners from two top US venture firms, Benchmark and Founders Fund, say they are uncomfortable with the current funding frenzy because it reminds them of 2021.

“I’m very uncomfortable.....because it feels like we're getting back to a point where prices have become untethered

items.title: As someone who has long been interested, and at time even obsessed, with pre-WW1 French political, cultural and economic history, I have read a great ...
items.description_html:
As someone who has long been interested, and at time even obsessed, with pre-WW1 French political, cultural and economic history, I have read a great deal about the Dreyfus Affair and have been as bewildered as anyone else about the passions it unleashed.

In this very interesting essay, Ruth Harris argues that the Dreyfus Affair was the first major example of a modern culture war, in which facts and evidence never mattered so much as which side of an existential divide you were on, and about which we are so familiar today.

I am not sure I agree that it was the first such example. After all even in France the Panama Scandal and the Boulanger Affair preceded the Dreyfus Affair, and had very similar casts of characters, and the US has an even longer history of political battles becoming culture wars, for example the bank wars under Andrew Jackson.

Still, the lessons of the Dreyfus Affair, as Harris explains, are very relevant for today's interminable and seemingly-existential political conflicts, and explain why facts often become unimportant in these battles.
https://drb.ie/article/rereadings-2-dreyfus-returns/

items.title: For decades India Defence story have been a manufacturing story where licence production, import substitution, domestic assembly have been the primary...
items.description_html:
For decades India Defence story have been a manufacturing story where licence production, import substitution, domestic assembly have been the primary talk of the town

But now the talks are incrementally moving towards new chapter i.e. who owns the IP and not just the factory.

This changes the economics you work upon where manufacturer competes on cost and capacity whereas for IP owner has to compete on R&D depth and gets paid for something that no one else can replicate easily.

New TAMs that are opening up across this theme in India are :-

Modern conflict is increasingly a contest of sensor-to-shooter loop speed and how fast you can detect, decide, and neutralize. Drones, AI-enabled autonomy, and electronic warfare compress that loop. Legacy platforms don't.

This is creating genuinely new addressable markets rather than just growing old ones:

> Tactical drones: procurement moving from ~INR30-35bn in the last cycle to INR120-140bn now
> Strategic drones: a separate ~INR300bn opportunity emerging alongside
> Counter-drone / anti-drone systems: multiple speakers independently flagged this as potentially as large as or larger than the offensive drone market itself
> Directed energy (high-power lasers, EMP): still nascent, but multiple players are already fielding real contracts (₹2bn-scale laser air-defense deals)

Nobody disputes that electronics is the value core of modern defense - C4ISR, software-defined radios, missile electronics, command-and-control. What's new is where the frontier of electronics is moving:

> AI-enabled mission software and autonomous decision systems
> Quantum computing - described as a national-level opportunity that could exceed 10bn in ecosystem investment
> Cybersecurity as its own dedicated vertical, not a bolt-on
> Space electronics as a genuinely new business line, not an adjacency

Semiconductors remain the biggest import dependency, accounting for the overwhelming majority of imported content even in otherwise highly indigenized electronics products.

Also now shipbuilding story is moving from defence to commercial shipbuilding. India pays ~US$85-100bn annually in freight costs, and roughly 85% of that goes to foreign shipping companies. Logistics costs sit at 13-14% of GDP. That's the actual policy driver and not military ambition, but plugging a massive structural outflow.

India wants to build the industrial base that captures value across the entire maritime chain >>> shipping, shipbuilding, finance, arbitration and not just build hulls.

And then there is this space economy that is emerging currently in India. India's space sector opened to private participation in 2020, the number of private space companies has gone from ~7 to over 300.

The military angle compounds it where the government's plan is for 51 military satellites represents less than 20% of India's estimated long-term requirement. Satellite constellations need a minimum of ~7 satellites for continuous coverage

On the commercial launch side, the economics are the story and reusability is the thing to watch. Current break-even launch costs sit around US$10,000/kg; recovery-enabled reusable systems are targeting below US$4,000/kg. That's the same cost curve logic that made reusability transformative in global launch markets, now playing out domestically, with genuinely indigenous technology (single-piece 3D-printed engines manufactured in one print cycle, for instance) rather than licensed tech.

Space is also quietly merging into defense electronics companies building satellite/space-grade electronics are the same companies building EW and radar systems. It's becoming one continuous capability stack rather than two separate industries.

items.title: RT Mayor Zohran Kwame Mamdani: Today marks 250 years since a small group of newspaper editors, farmers, and soldiers signed a document declaring our n...
items.description_html:
RT Mayor Zohran Kwame Mamdani
Today marks 250 years since a small group of newspaper editors, farmers, and soldiers signed a document declaring our nation’s independence — a truth that feels self-evident now but was revolutionary then.

What a privilege us Americans have, to live in a nation that every one of its inhabitants can shape. What an honor us New Yorkers have, to look out over our city’s waters from the shores where so many Americans bravely entered their country for the first time.

Today and all days, let us remember that patriotism is not pretending our nation is without flaws. Patriotism is every act of righteous dissent — because loving our country means fighting for the best version of it.

Happy Fourth of July, New York City.

items.title: SCMP: "In recent years, the central government has been trying to ease residency restrictions. However, analysts warned that local authorities might h...
items.description_html:
SCMP: "In recent years, the central government has been trying to ease residency restrictions. However, analysts warned that local authorities might have different priorities and be reluctant to implement the changes for financial reasons."

This, in a nutshell, is why rebalancing is so difficult. By limiting legal residence rights and by reducing schooling, pension, medical and other social transfers, the hukou system is part of the structural reasons for the very low household share of China's GDP.

This low household share, in turn, explains China's very low consumption, its manufacturing competitiveness, and its reliance on overinvestment, surging debt and trade surpluses to keep growth from dropping sharply.

So why not eliminate the hukou system, as Beijing has been promising for years? Almost everyone agrees this would be good both for Chinese workers and the Chinese economy.

But there is a problem. Transfers benefit certain sectors of the economy at the expense of other sectors, and if the former are important, or politically powerful, eliminating those transfers can be highly disruptive in the short term.

The transfers implicit in the hukou system have for decades benefitted businesses (by keeping wages low) and local governments (by reducing social expenditures), and in so doing have subsidized the increases in economic activity needed to meet Beijing's excessively high GDP growth targets.

If eliminating the hukou means that local governments have to expand the services they now grant only to hukou residents, this expansion has to be financed, either by reducing overall services, taxing households, taxing businesses or (temporarily) expanding debt. It might also allow migrant workers to demand higher pay.

Because these all have significant political and/or economic costs in the short term , the best solution has been to talk about hukou reform but to implement it as slowly as possible in the hopes that something will turn up.

The hukou is only one of the many implicit and explicit transfer that keep the household share of China's economy very low, but they all suffer from the same implementation problem: raising the household share of GDP means reducing either the business share or the government share, and until you are willing to implement the latter, you can only talk about implementing the former.
https://sc.mp/otmw8?utm_source=twitter&utm_campaign=3359359&utm_medium=share_widget

I would add that productivity-enhancing technology is always a good thing as long as the growth in wages (which drive total demand) keep pace with the growth in productivity. In that case employment might shift from sectors with more productivity growth to sectors with less growth, but overall employment will be maintained.

items.title: META saying it is getting into the AI cloud business (to compete against Amazon's AWS & Microsoft's Azure) may be the most interesting new wrinkle in ...
items.description_html:
META saying it is getting into the AI cloud business (to compete against Amazon's AWS & Microsoft's Azure) may be the most interesting new wrinkle in the AI capex debate !

Here is why,

The bull case - is that this is not Meta backing down on AI spends. It is Meta trying to make shareholders more comfortable with massive capex by showing that unused or lower-priority compute can be monetised. In this reading, a potential 'MetaAWS' is a sign of a more sophisticated AI infra market - not the end of AI demand.

The bear case - If Meta is looking to rent out compute, is it because Zuckerberg has built ahead of actual internal demand ? That would make this an early warning signal for the AI capex chain - GPUs, memory, networking, packaging & semi equipment.

In a way the question isn't really whether Meta is spending too much money - it is - whether Meta is monetising abundance ? OR this is an admission of excess ?

For India, this debate matters as we all well know. India isn't part of the so called 'AI-trade'.. which has sucked in global equity flows for 2 years now. Consensus view is that as this trade begins to wobble, perhaps global capital would then start to look at markets like India ?

Weigh in with what you think.

#AI #Nifty #BankNifty #Technology #India #ITStocks

@CNBCTV18News

items.title: The biggest shift in early-stage venture funding is that the bar for raising a Series A has become materially higher. "The benchmarks that you need to...
items.description_html:
The biggest shift in early-stage venture funding is that the bar for raising a Series A has become materially higher.

"The benchmarks that you need to hit to raise a Series A are just much higher," says Rajan Anandan of Peak XV Partners, adding that raising $1–2 million at the seed stage may no longer be enough to reach those milestones.

- AI startups may need several million dollars in ARR before attracting institutional Series A investors, while the cost of hiring top AI talent continues to rise.

- Consumer brands may need a clear path to a Rs 70–100 crore revenue run rate before they can attract meaningful follow-on capital.

This is one reason why Peak XV's seed-stage programme, Surge, has increased its maximum initial investment to $5 million, up from $1.5 million when it launched in 2019.

More here: https://www.thearcweb.com/article/peak-xv-surge-rajan-anandan-seed-iHmq9aXJYgEDrIP8

items.title: When Siddhartha Lal took charge of Royal Enfield, many people inside the industry were skeptical. Most CEOs would have treated the motorcycle division...
items.description_html:
When Siddhartha Lal took charge of Royal Enfield, many people inside the industry were skeptical.

Most CEOs would have treated the motorcycle division as a side project.

Instead, Lal did something unusual.

He became deeply obsessed with the motorcycles themselves.

He rode them personally.
He spoke directly with riders.
He studied what made people emotionally attached to the brand.

What he discovered shaped the company’s strategy.

Royal Enfield riders didn’t want:
the fastest bikes
the lightest bikes
the most technologically advanced bikes.

They wanted something much rarer. They wanted soul.

So Lal made a radical decision:

Royal Enfield would not compete with Japanese bikes on performance. Instead, it would build motorcycles around heritage, simplicity, and character.

This philosophy would later become known as “Pure Motorcycling.”

RT Sparsh Agarwal
Glad to make this public!

items.title: RT West Bengal Diary: 🚄FROM VANDE BHARAT TO KOLKATA TRAMS: JUPITER WAGONS’ BIG BET ON BENGAL’S RAIL FUTURE!🚋 One of India’s leading railway m...
items.description_html:
RT West Bengal Diary
🚄FROM VANDE BHARAT TO KOLKATA TRAMS: JUPITER WAGONS’ BIG BET ON BENGAL’S RAIL FUTURE!🚋

One of India’s leading railway manufacturers, Jupiter Wagons, is making a major leap into passenger rail—and West Bengal is at the centre of its expansion plans.

🏭 New Hooghly coach factory: Jupiter Wagons will establish a dedicated manufacturing facility in Hooghly to build Vande Bharat trainsets, Metro coaches and other passenger rolling stock, making it Bengal’s second Vande Bharat coach manufacturing hub after Titagarh Rail Systems’ Uttarpara plant.

🚆 Entering the Vande Bharat era: The company is expanding beyond freight wagons to compete in India’s rapidly growing passenger rail market, targeting upcoming Vande Bharat, Metro and suburban train opportunities.

🌍 Global technology partnerships: Jupiter is working with international partners, including an existing collaboration with CAF (Spain), as it prepares to bid for major passenger rail projects, including Mumbai’s ₹20,000 crore suburban rail programme.

⚙️ Building the complete ecosystem: A new wheelset manufacturing facility in Odisha, developed with international partners, will supply critical wheels and axles for passenger coaches while also serving export markets.

🚋 Kolkata trams could benefit too: The company has expressed interest in participating in the modernisation of Kolkata’s historic tram network, should the state’s revival plans move ahead.

📈 Ambitious growth plans: Jupiter Wagons is targeting annual revenues of ₹10,000 crore over the next few years, with passenger rail expected to become a major growth driver.

If these plans materialise, West Bengal will strengthen its position as one of India’s leading railway manufacturing centres—producing everything from freight wagons and Metro coaches to Vande Bharat trains and potentially even modern trams.

#WestBengal #VandeBharat #Railways

items.title: I finally have an announcement to make! I've been creating content for almost a decade, and so many of you have told me my work has impacted your inve...
items.description_html:
I finally have an announcement to make!

I've been creating content for almost a decade, and so many of you have told me my work has impacted your investing in some way. That means everything to me!

So a little over a year ago I decided to formalize things and got SEBI-registered. For a finance content creator already doing well, you might ask why add all that compliance and complexity. Simple, I felt quality stock market research that is also accountable was missing.

A lot of you in my circle have heard me mention an app I've been building. Since then I've built a team of around 20, mostly in research (fundamental and technical), plus tech, customer success, and operations.

Together, we built @RecoWealth, a platform and community for Quality Equity Research, Curated Stock Recommendations with Transparency, and a genuine community around all things investing in India and US.

We think most people are underexposed to equity in general, and even more so to global markets.

We didn't want to be just another place for stock tips so we've vertically integrated to be with you from the start of your investing journey and as a partner throughout.

Time to change the way people look at Research! We are coming soon with our App! Stay Tuned.....

items.title: RT Moses Harding John: Now that I am seeing the Life & General Insurance businesses closely, I notice that distributors walk away with big chunk of th...
items.description_html:
RT Moses Harding John
Now that I am seeing the Life & General Insurance businesses closely, I notice that distributors walk away with big chunk of the cake while the product manufacturers struggle to “contain” claim settlements (adjusted for reinsurance) & operating costs to stay in profit after adjusting the losses from core business against income from investment portfolio!

Cost of acquisition (distribution fees) + reinsurance + claim settlements + operating cost is always >100% net premium (gross premium adjusted for distribution fees + reinsurance) which is a funny business model! The profitability is high dependent on the performance of the investment portfolio, like an AMC!

Banks owning LI & GI (that includes health) businesses subsidize the cost of acquisition through their branch network to stay ahead in the race!

IRDAI needs to set up a fair play between the companies & distributors to make the insurance sector thrive & be customer efficient!

Coverage of risks around life & health and movable & immovable assets is as critical as banking & financial services sector, but unfortunately not provided the desired regulatory attention beyond governance & compliance!

Insurance sector is like a (life & non-life) risk-underwriting AMC!

items.title: At anchor in the Persian Gulf, Abhijit Chopra found out about the US-Iran peace deal when his phone lit up with messages from family and friends. The ...
items.description_html:
At anchor in the Persian Gulf, Abhijit Chopra found out about the US-Iran peace deal when his phone lit up with messages from family and friends. The captain of a crude oil tanker, he had to temper his excitement. There were no signs of celebration from nearby vessels, and no ships making haste towards the Strait of Hormuz.

Chopra and his 21-strong crew have been trapped since the war began in late February. At the beginning, they struggled with fear and uncertainty, which ebbed into boredom and a constant battle not to let negative thoughts set in. For more than 120 days they have waited, dining together and bonding by singing old Hindi songs at karaoke.

Full story
https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-07-03/strait-of-hormuz-sailors-remain-trapped-despite-iran-us-ceasefire

items.title: RT Press Trust of India: VIDEO | A remarkable piece of heritage has been restored in Karnataka's Belagavi district. A 300-year-old stepwell, which had...
items.description_html:
RT Press Trust of India
VIDEO | A remarkable piece of heritage has been restored in Karnataka's Belagavi district. A 300-year-old stepwell, which had been buried under garbage and debris, has been brought back to life.

The 80-feet-deep stepwell, believed to have been built during the Adil Shahi dynasty between the 15th and 17th century, has now been fully restored by the Belagavi-based Pyaas Foundation with the support of NSS volunteers and local residents. It reveals 53 stone steps, and a unique layout that looks like a Shiva Lingam when viewed from above.

#Belagavi #KarnatakaNews

(Full video available on PTI Videos - http://ptivideos.com)

items.title: RT Maheshwer Peri: There’s a particular kind of fear that only a parent knows. The fear that comes watching your child choose a life you can’t quite...
items.description_html:
RT Maheshwer Peri
There’s a particular kind of fear that only a parent knows. The fear that comes watching your child choose a life you can’t quite protect them.

Vinayak is an engineer by education. He was in product, good at his job, well liked and adding value. Everything about it looked right from where I stood: Security, salary, work place etc.

But he felt that he didn’t fit. Not as a complaint but a quiet truth. He didn’t like the closed spaces, the stillness and the daily routine.

On a family holiday, he took to deep sea diving. He went back again, to the same diving site. He learnt. He trained. And he started interning with them and helping tourists. He would check the oxygen cylinders, give demos, instructions. And he soon got certification as a deep sea diving instructor.

And one fine day, he announced that he is quitting from his corporate life. He went abroad and learnt more. And more. And more. He trained. Got every certification - On diving, surviving etc.

He is now a deep sea diving instructor, working in Indonesia. He earls less than half the pay, carries cylinders, spends long hours underwater, lives without the comforts I spent a career trying to provide. No AC room. Not even a wash basin in his room. No safety net.

He is happy. Underwater, undercompensated, and, by every real measure, more himself than he ever was in that office.

My fear hasn’t fully gone. I don’t think it’s meant to. He didn’t need me to protect him from this choice. He needed me to trust that he knew himself better than I did. That’s the harder thing to learn, as a parent: sometimes love means stepping back and letting someone be honest, brave and trusted.

I am proud. He had the courage that I didn’t have. He gave up comforts and chose what his heart said. He trained. Learnt. He was happy to be a subordinate than be the promoters son. He chose the difficult path. The not so treaded path. He is happy. And I am proud.

Happy birthday Vinayak.

items.title: Bloomberg: Since Bretton Woods cemented the global dominance of the dollar in 1944, "Americans and the world have been arguing about it. Some US analy...
items.description_html:
Bloomberg: Since Bretton Woods cemented the global dominance of the dollar in 1944, "Americans and the world have been arguing about it. Some US analysts worry about losing that status; a few think it’s not worth all the trouble. Plenty of non-Americans resent the dollar’s dominance; no one can put forward a better alternative."

Count me among those who thinks US dollar dominance is definitely not worth the trouble, but who also believe that its dominance will remain as long as the US is wiling (and credibly able), for political reasons, to embrace an overvalued dollar that sacrifices the interest of its manufacturers, workers and middle classes for the benefit of Wall Street, the US foreign affairs establishment, and mercantilist economies that need to acquire foreign assets to balance their trade surpluses.
https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-07-02/can-the-us-dollar-remain-the-world-s-reserve-currency?utm_source=website&utm_medium=share&utm_campaign=twitter

items.title: RT Intrinsic Compounding: On 30th June 2026, the Expenditure Finance Committee cleared a Rs 1.25 trillion outlay for ISM 2.0, against Rs 760bn for ISM...
items.description_html:
RT Intrinsic Compounding
On 30th June 2026, the Expenditure Finance Committee cleared a Rs 1.25 trillion outlay for ISM 2.0, against Rs 760bn for ISM 1.0. Union Cabinet approval is expected shortly. But the number is the smaller part of the story.

ISM 1.0 was a manufacturing-enablement scheme: get plants built, prove India can execute at scale and quality, put a 50% central capex subsidy behind fabs, OSAT and display units, disbursed pari-passu with capital deployment rather than tied to production targets like a PLI scheme.

That single design choice, government co-investing from day one instead of paying out after commissioning, is what let India move from approval to construction faster than most Western programmes.

And now ISM 2.0 is altogether a different story where it adds three new pillars on top of the manufacturing base: equipment and materials, IP and deeptech development, and talent at scale.

ECMS, the components and materials scheme, has been doubled to Rs 400bn. Basic customs duty has been removed on 25 critical minerals, lithium, cobalt, germanium among them, the first real move toward an indigenous materials base. On IP, the scheme is now explicitly targeting full-stack Indian chip design as a stated goal, DPIIT recognition for semiconductor startups has been extended to 20 years, and a datacentre tax holiday runs to 2047. On talent, the target is 85,000 industry-ready engineers by CY27, a workforce of process engineers, metrology specialists and cleanroom technicians that India's 3 lakh chip designers, excellent for GCC-style design work, simply cannot supply on their own. In short: ISM 1.0 proved India could build the box. ISM 2.0 is trying to make sure India doesn't have to import everything that goes inside it.

ISM was never trying to win at the leading edge; sub-7nm logic is absent from the roadmap and will stay absent for a long time. Instead it targets 28-110nm logic and OSAT, segments that together account for roughly 56% of global wafer capacity and cover almost everything India's own auto, industrial and consumer electronics actually consume.

India's cost base in OSAT runs an estimated ~45% below Taiwan on an indexed basis and ~10% below Malaysia, already one of the most competitive geographies globally.

Combine that with a design workforce that is roughly a fifth of the world's chip-design talent pool, second only to the US, and you get a genuinely differentiated entry point: cheap assembly, deep design talent, and a captive domestic demand base of $62bn today headed to $155bn by CY31 as electronics production scales toward the government's $500bn FY31 target.

Across design, India's presence is really two layers :- the GCC arm of global majors doing serious front-end work (Intel's ~$9bn design centre, Qualcomm's team that did the Feb'26 2nm tape-out, AMD's largest global design centre in Bengaluru, plus NXP, Nvidia, Marvell, Renesas, Texas Instruments) sitting alongside an early but genuine domestic fabless layer (Aura Semiconductor in analog/mixed-signal, Mindgrove in RISC-V SoCs, Saankhya Labs in SDR/5G broadcast backed by Tata's Tejas Networks, InCore Semiconductors spun off IIT Madras's SHAKTI processor programme, Silizium Circuits and Agnit in GaN, iVP Semiconductor in power discretes, Netrasemi in edge-AI chiplets);

In wafer fabrication India has exactly one project, the Tata Electronics-PSMC fab at Dholera targeting 28-110nm and first chip in Dec'26; OSAT/ATMP is where the actual manufacturing muscle is concentrated Micron's Sanand ATMP (DRAM/NAND, operational), Kaynes Semicon at Sanand (IPMs for EVs, operational), CG Power's CG Semi at Sanand (automotive/industrial packages, G1 live, G2 by end-CY26), Tata Electronics' Jagiroad OSAT in Assam (largest single OSAT bet, phase-1 commissioning), the HCL Group-Foxconn JV at Jewar (targeting CY28), plus a newer batch approved in Aug'25/May'26, 3D Glass Solutions and SiCSem in Odisha, Continental Device in Punjab, Advanced System in Package in Andhra Pradesh, Crystal Matrix and Suchi Semicon in Gujarat;

And upstream in equipment/materials, still the weakest layer, you've got Linde India, INOX Air Products and INOX India building out gas infrastructure at Dholera, Navin Fluorine and Gujarat Fluorochemicals moving into electronic-grade acids, Deepak Nitrite/Deepak Fertilisers into electronic-grade IPA, Aether Industries and Tatva Chintan and Jubilant Ingrevia into specialty semicon chemicals, Thermax/VA Tech Wabag/Praj Industries in ultrapure water systems, Carborundum Universal in fab-equipment ceramics, Voltas in cleanroom HVAC (already executing for Tata Electronics), and Siemens supplying automation into fab and OSAT plants.

Nearly all of this last group is still at the trial-order or MoU stage rather than committed fab contracts, which is precisely the gap ISM 2.0's new equipment-and-materials pillar is trying to force closed.

But this can be a interesting turn to Indian Semicon space in general

IT is up. Everyone is looking for a news or reason. Maybe it's just the realization that pessmism is running ahead of reality (tweeted this yesterday also).

Allied reading:
https://www.ndtvprofit.com/technology/palantir-ceo-karp-slams-anthropic-openai-token-pricing-something-has-gone-completely-wrong-11715871
Strong remarks made by Palantir CEO Alex Karp yesterday. criticized the token-based pricing approach used by AI labs like Anthropic and OpenAI, arguing that “something has gone completely wrong.” He said rising AI costs and newer, pricier model generations are frustrating enterprise customers, leading them to avoid “tokenmaxxing” (spending primarily based on tokens) and instead focus on clearer return on investment.

Karp suggested enterprises may shift toward open-weight models that can deliver similar capabilities at lower cost, and he highlighted concerns that China is advancing rapidly in AI development. He also emphasized a trend away from broad, general-purpose AI and toward more efficient, proprietary tools tailored to specific businesses.

In line with Palantir’s broader stance, Karp positioned open-weight approaches (including Palantir’s work with Nvidia) as a solution—framing the key need for buyers as control over compute, models, and data (the “means of production”), rather than handing that control to external providers.
